data.title: Is Kratos (KTOS) Using Its 5G NTN Win To Recast Its Defense-Centric Technology Narrative?
data.description:

  • This contract highlights Kratos’ role in bringing satellites into mainstream mobile networks, potentially broadening its relevance beyond traditional defense into commercial communications infrastructure.

To own Kratos, you have to believe its mix of defense unmanned systems and software-based space infrastructure can justify today’s premium valuation and modest current margins. The SKY Perfect JSAT 5G-NTN win reinforces the space and communications side of that thesis, but it does not materially change the near term catalyst around converting its large defense pipeline into funded production, nor the key risk that heavy upfront CapEx and working capital continue to weigh on free cash flow.

The Airbus Uncrewed Collaborative Combat Aircraft program, built around the Valkyrie, is especially relevant here because it shows Kratos trying to balance capital intensive drone production with more software and systems work like OpenSpace. While Airbus and Valkyrie speak directly to the upside case in unmanned platforms, the SKY Perfect JSAT announcement highlights that OpenSpace and 5G-NTN could become a meaningful second pillar for growth, offering some diversification if large defense awards slip.

Why the DG Matrix partnership matters for Infineon Technologies stock

Infineon Technologies (XTRA:IFX) is in focus after DG Matrix agreed to use its latest-generation silicon carbide MOSFETs in the Interport solid-state transformer platform for AI data centers and electrification projects worldwide.

This agreement links Infineon directly to power infrastructure for AI workloads and utility microgrids, an area investors increasingly watch when assessing how chipmakers are positioned across data center and energy transition themes.

See our latest analysis for Infineon Technologies.

Most Popular Narrative: 21.2% Undervalued

According to the most followed narrative, Infineon’s fair value of €50.32 sits well above the last close at €39.66, setting up a clear valuation gap that investors will want to understand before deciding how this AI power angle fits in their portfolio.

The balance sheet looks very healthy, basically the company is owned by the share holders (equity €17.2b), only a reasonable low share is held by the “banks” (debt €5.4b). Having that in mind, a ROE of 17,5% is a good result, because it would be easy to raise ROE to higher figure, by the leverage effect of debt: IFX could sell more products by increasing the production capacity, that would lead to more result and this would directly increase ROE. The money for more production capacity could be provided from the “banks”, which leads to more debt. That’s the leverage, more debt (at cost x), significant more result (result at 2-5 times x). But IFX operates very moderate, with low debt compared to equity. This leads to a healthy debt to equity ratio of 31,3%.

Approach 1: SOLV Energy Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

Approach 2: SOLV Energy Price vs Earnings (P/E)

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful check on value because it links what you pay per share to the earnings that each share generates. It gives you a simple way to see how many dollars investors are currently paying for one dollar of earnings.

A "normal" or "fair" P/E typically reflects what investors expect from a company’s future earnings growth and how much risk they see in those earnings. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower P/E.

SOLV Energy currently trades on a P/E of about 22.79x. That compares with a Construction industry average P/E of around 37.42x and a peer group average of about 26.88x, so the stock is pricing earnings at a lower level than both of those benchmarks.
